description:
You are working as a junior data-analyst.  
    A CSV file containing customer information already exists at:

        /home/user/data/clients.csv

    The CSV has the following exact header row (comma-separated, *no surrounding spaces*):

        id,name,email,status

    Your goal is to produce a JSON file that lists only the customers whose **status** column is exactly the word “active” (all lower-case).  

    Requirements for the JSON file:

    1. Create a new directory  
           /home/user/output  
       if it does not already exist.

    2. Inside that directory, create a file named  
           /home/user/output/active_clients.json

    3. The JSON must be a single top-level array.  
       • Each element is an object containing exactly these three keys, in this order:  
           1) "id"   (number, not string)  
           2) "name" (string)  
           3) "email" (string)  

       • Do **not** include the original CSV “status” column in the objects.  
       • Keep the objects in the same order they appear in the source CSV.  
       • Use two-space indentation for each nested level.  
       • The file must end with a single trailing newline (Unix LF).

    4. Accomplish the whole extraction and conversion with **one single shell command** (a pipeline counts as one command).  
       After running the command, verify that the JSON file exactly matches the required format.

    5. When everything is finished, you should be able to display the whole file with:  
           cat /home/user/output/active_clients.json

       and see something that looks exactly like:

           [
             {
               "id": 1,
               "name": "John Doe",
               "email": "john@example.com"
             },
             {
               "id": 3,
               "name": "Bob Lee",
               "email": "bob@example.com"
             }
           ]

       (including the closing square bracket, the indentation and the trailing newline).

    Do not hard-code the expected rows; your command should dynamically filter based on the “status” column.  
    Use whatever command-line utilities are available in a typical Linux environment (e.g. csvkit, jq, awk, etc.) but keep everything in a single command line.
truth:
<!-- Pre-existing file that the test harness creates before the agent starts -->
    <preexisting>
        <file path="/home/user/data/clients.csv"><![CDATA[id,name,email,status
1,John Doe,john@example.com,active
2,Jane Smith,jane@example.com,inactive
3,Bob Lee,bob@example.com,active
4,Alice Kim,alice@example.com,pending
]]></file>
    </preexisting>

    <!-- Expected artefacts after successful task completion -->
    <expected>
        <file path="/home/user/output/active_clients.json"><![CDATA[
[
  {
    "id": 1,
    "name": "John Doe",
    "email": "john@example.com"
  },
  {
    "id": 3,
    "name": "Bob Lee",
    "email": "bob@example.com"
  }
]
]]></file>
    </expected>
name: task_000000_c02b5da5
